question:
Find all pairs of polynomials $p(x)$ and $q(x)$ with real coefficients for which
\[
p(x) q(x+1) - p(x+1) q(x) = 1.
\]
solution:
\textbf{First solution.}
The pairs $(p,q)$ satisfying the given equation are those of the form $p(x) = ax+b, q(x) = cx+d$
for $a,b,c,d \in \RR$ such that $bc- ad = 1$. We will see later that these indeed give solutions.

Suppose $p$ and $q$ satisfy the given equation; note that neither $p$ nor $q$ can be identically zero.
By subtracting the equations
\begin{align*}
p(x) q(x+1) - p(x+1) q(x) &= 1 \\
p(x-1) q(x) - p(x) q(x-1) &= 1,
\end{align*}
we obtain the equation
\[
p(x) (q(x+1) + q(x-1)) = q(x) (p(x+1) + p(x-1)).
\]
The original equation implies that $p(x)$ and $q(x)$ have no common nonconstant factor,
so $p(x)$ divides $p(x+1) + p(x-1)$. Since each of $p(x+1)$ and $p(x-1)$ has the same degree and leading
coefficient as $p$, we must have
\[
p(x+1) + p(x-1) = 2p(x).
\]
If we define the polynomials $r(x) = p(x+1) - p(x)$, $s(x) = q(x+1) - q(x)$,
we have $r(x+1) = r(x)$, and similarly $s(x+1) = s(x)$.
Put
\[
a = r(0), b = p(0), c = s(0), d = q(0).
\]
Then $r(x) = a, s(x) = c$ for all $x \in \ZZ$, and hence identically;
consequently, $p(x) = ax + b, q(x) = cx + d$ for all $x \in \ZZ$, and hence identically.
For $p$ and $q$ of this form,
\[
p(x) q(x+1) - p(x+1) q(x) = bc - ad,
\]
so we get a solution if and only if $bc-ad=1$, as claimed.

\textbf{Second solution.}
(Communicated by Catalin Zara.)
Again, note that $p$ and $q$ must be nonzero.
Write
\begin{align*}
p(x) &= p_0 + p_1 x + \cdots + p_m x^m \\
q(x) &= q_0 + q_1 x + \cdots + q_n x^n
\end{align*}
with $p_m, q_n \neq 0$, so that $m = \deg(p), n = \deg(q)$. It is enough to derive a contradiction
assuming that $\max\{m,n\} > 1$, the remaining cases being treated as in the
first solution.

Put $R(x) = p(x) q(x+1) - p(x+1) q(x)$. Since $m+n \geq 2$ by assumption,
the coefficient of $x^{m+n-1}$ in $R(x)$
must vanish. By easy algebra, this coefficient equals $(m-n) p_m q_n$, so we must have $m=n > 1$.

For $k=1,\dots,2m-2$, the coefficient of $x^k$ in $R(x)$ is
\[
\sum_{i+j>k, j>i} \left( \binom{j}{k-i} - \binom{i}{k-j} \right)(p_i q_j - p_j q_i)
\]
and must vanish.
For $k=2m-2$, the only summand is for $(i,j) = (m-1,m)$, so $p_{m-1} q_m = p_m q_{m-1}$.

Suppose now that $h \geq 1$ and that $p_i q_j = p_j q_i$ is known to vanish whenever
$j>i \geq h$. (By the previous paragraph, we initially have this for $h=m-1$.)
Take $k = m+h-2$ and note that the conditions $i+j > h, j \leq m$ force $i \geq h-1$.
Using the hypothesis, we see that the only possible nonzero contribution to the coefficient of $x^k$ in $R(x)$
is from $(i,j) = (h-1,m)$. Hence $p_{h-1} q_m = p_m q_{h-1}$; since $p_m, q_m \neq 0$, this implies
$p_{h-1} q_j = p_j q_{h-1}$ whenever $j > h-1$.

By descending induction, we deduce that $p_i q_j = p_j q_i$ whenever $j>i \geq 0$. Consequently,
$p(x)$ and $q(x)$ are scalar multiples of each other, forcing $R(x) = 0$, a contradiction.

\textbf{Third solution.}
(Communicated by David Feldman.)
As in the second solution, we note that there are no solutions where $m = \deg(p), n = \deg(q)$
are distinct and $m+n \geq 2$. Suppose $p,q$ form a solution with $m = n \geq 2$.
The desired identity asserts that the matrix
\[
\begin{pmatrix}
p(x) & p(x+1) \\
q(x) & q(x+1)
\end{pmatrix}
\]
has determinant 1. This condition is preserved by replacing $q(x)$ with $q(x) - tp(x)$ for any
real number $t$. In particular, we can choose $t$ so that $\deg(q(x) - tp(x)) < m$;
we then obtain a contradiction.

descriptive_long.question:
Find all pairs of polynomials $polyone(realvar)$ and $polytwo(realvar)$ with real coefficients for which
\[
polyone(realvar)\,polytwo(realvar+1)-polyone(realvar+1)\,polytwo(realvar)=1.
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
extbf{First solution.}
The pairs $(polyone,polytwo)$ satisfying the given equation are those of the form $polyone(realvar)=constalpha\,realvar+constbeta$, $polytwo(realvar)=constgamma\,realvar+constdelta$ for $constalpha,constbeta,constgamma,constdelta\in\RR$ such that $constbeta constgamma-constalpha constdelta=1$. We will see later that these indeed give solutions.

Suppose polyone and polytwo satisfy the given equation; note that neither polyone nor polytwo can be identically zero. By subtracting the equations
\[
polyone(realvar)\,polytwo(realvar+1)-polyone(realvar+1)\,polytwo(realvar)=1,\
polyone(realvar-1)\,polytwo(realvar)-polyone(realvar)\,polytwo(realvar-1)=1,
\] we obtain
\[
polyone(realvar) igl(polytwo(realvar+1)+polytwo(realvar-1) igr)=polytwo(realvar) igl(polyone(realvar+1)+polyone(realvar-1) igr).
\]The original equation implies that $polyone(realvar)$ and $polytwo(realvar)$ have no common nonconstant factor, so $polyone(realvar)$ divides $polyone(realvar+1)+polyone(realvar-1)$. Since each of $polyone(realvar+1)$ and $polyone(realvar-1)$ has the same degree and leading coefficient as $polyone$, we must have
\[
polyone(realvar+1)+polyone(realvar-1)=2\,polyone(realvar).
\]
Define $diffpoly(realvar)=polyone(realvar+1)-polyone(realvar)$ and $diffpolyq(realvar)=polytwo(realvar+1)-polytwo(realvar)$; then $diffpoly(realvar+1)=diffpoly(realvar)$ and $diffpolyq(realvar+1)=diffpolyq(realvar)$. Put
\[
constalpha=diffpoly(0),\quad constbeta=polyone(0),\quad constgamma=diffpolyq(0),\quad constdelta=polytwo(0).
\]Hence $diffpoly(realvar)=constalpha$ and $diffpolyq(realvar)=constgamma$ identically, so $polyone(realvar)=constalpha\,realvar+constbeta$ and $polytwo(realvar)=constgamma\,realvar+constdelta$. For such polynomials
\[
polyone(realvar)\,polytwo(realvar+1)-polyone(realvar+1)\,polytwo(realvar)=constbeta constgamma-constalpha constdelta,
\]so a solution occurs exactly when $constbeta constgamma-constalpha constdelta=1$.

	extbf{Second solution.}
Again, polyone and polytwo must be non-zero. Write
\[
 egin{aligned}
polyone(realvar)&=p_0+p_1 realvar+\cdots+leadcoeffp\,realvar^{degreep},\
polytwo(realvar)&=q_0+q_1 realvar+\cdots+leadcoeffq\,realvar^{degreeq},
\end{aligned}
\]with $leadcoeffp,leadcoeffq
eq0$, so $degreep=\deg(polyone)$ and $degreeq=\deg(polytwo)$. Assume $\max\{degreep,degreeq\}>1$; the remaining cases are settled by the first solution.

Put $resultant(realvar)=polyone(realvar)\,polytwo(realvar+1)-polyone(realvar+1)\,polytwo(realvar)$. Because $degreep+degreeq\ge2$, the coefficient of $realvar^{degreep+degreeq-1}$ in $resultant(realvar)$ vanishes. A direct calculation gives this coefficient as $(degreep-degreeq)leadcoeffp leadcoeffq$, forcing $degreep=degreeq>1$.

For $indexthr=1,\dots,2degreep-2$, the coefficient of $realvar^{indexthr}$ in $resultant(realvar)$ is
\[
\sum_{indexone+indextwo>indexthr,\;indextwo>indexone}\Bigl( inom{indextwo}{indexthr-indexone}- inom{indexone}{indexthr-indextwo}\Bigr) igl(coeffpi\,coeffqj-p_{indextwo}q_{indexone} igr),
\]and must be zero. For $indexthr=2degreep-2$ the only summand is $(indexone,indextwo)=(degreep-1,degreep)$, giving $prevcoefp\,q_{degreep}=leadcoeffp\,prevcoefq$.

Now suppose $indexfor\ge1$ and that $p_{indexone}q_{indextwo}=p_{indextwo}q_{indexone}$ whenever $indextwo>indexone\ge indexfor$ (initially true for $indexfor=degreep-1$). Taking $indexthr=degreep+indexfor-2$, the conditions $indexone+indextwo>indexfor$ and $indextwo\le degreep$ force $indexone\ge indexfor-1$. The only potential contribution to the coefficient of $realvar^{indexthr}$ then comes from $(indexone,indextwo)=(indexfor-1,degreep)$, giving $prevcoefph\,q_{degreep}=leadcoeffp\,prevcoefqh$. Because $leadcoeffp,q_{degreep}
eq0$, we deduce $p_{indexfor-1}q_{indextwo}=p_{indextwo}q_{indexfor-1}$ whenever $indextwo>indexfor-1$.

Descending induction yields $p_{indexone}q_{indextwo}=p_{indextwo}q_{indexone}$ whenever $indextwo>indexone\ge0$. Thus polyone and polytwo are scalar multiples, so $resultant(realvar)=0$, contradicting the given identity.

	extbf{Third solution.}
As above, there are no solutions with distinct degrees when $degreep+degreeq\ge2$. Assume $degreep=degreeq\ge2$. The identity asserts that the matrix
\[
 egin{pmatrix}
polyone(realvar)&polyone(realvar+1)\[4pt]
polytwo(realvar)&polytwo(realvar+1)
\end{pmatrix}
\]has determinant 1. Replacing $polytwo(realvar)$ by $polytwo(realvar)-paramtee\,polyone(realvar)$ for any real $paramtee$ preserves the determinant. Choosing $paramtee$ so that $\deg igl(polytwo(realvar)-paramtee\,polyone(realvar) igr)<degreep$ gives a contradiction, completing the proof.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Find all pairs of polynomials $tornadoes(hummingbird)$ and $rainstorm(hummingbird)$ with real coefficients for which
\[
tornadoes(hummingbird) rainstorm(hummingbird+1) - tornadoes(hummingbird+1) rainstorm(hummingbird) = 1.
\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
\textbf{First solution.}
The pairs $(tornadoes,rainstorm)$ satisfying the given equation are those of the form $tornadoes(hummingbird) = pinecone\,hummingbird + horseshoe,\; rainstorm(hummingbird) = lilypond\,hummingbird + snowflake$ for $pinecone, horseshoe, lilypond, snowflake \in \RR$ such that $horseshoe\,lilypond - pinecone\,snowflake = 1$. We will see later that these indeed give solutions.

Suppose $tornadoes$ and $rainstorm$ satisfy the given equation; note that neither $tornadoes$ nor $rainstorm$ can be identically zero.
By subtracting the equations
\begin{align*}
tornadoes(hummingbird)\,rainstorm(hummingbird+1) - tornadoes(hummingbird+1)\,rainstorm(hummingbird) &= 1 \\
tornadoes(hummingbird-1)\,rainstorm(hummingbird) - tornadoes(hummingbird)\,rainstorm(hummingbird-1) &= 1,
\end{align*}
we obtain the equation
\[
tornadoes(hummingbird)\bigl(rainstorm(hummingbird+1)+rainstorm(hummingbird-1)\bigr)=rainstorm(hummingbird)\bigl(tornadoes(hummingbird+1)+tornadoes(hummingbird-1)\bigr).
\]
The original equation implies that $tornadoes(hummingbird)$ and $rainstorm(hummingbird)$ have no common nonconstant factor, so $tornadoes(hummingbird)$ divides $tornadoes(hummingbird+1)+tornadoes(hummingbird-1)$. Since each of $tornadoes(hummingbird+1)$ and $tornadoes(hummingbird-1)$ has the same degree and leading coefficient as $tornadoes$, we must have
\[
tornadoes(hummingbird+1)+tornadoes(hummingbird-1)=2\,tornadoes(hummingbird).
\]
If we define the polynomials $shipwreck(hummingbird)=tornadoes(hummingbird+1)-tornadoes(hummingbird)$, $bricklayer(hummingbird)=rainstorm(hummingbird+1)-rainstorm(hummingbird)$, we have $shipwreck(hummingbird+1)=shipwreck(hummingbird)$ and $bricklayer(hummingbird+1)=bricklayer(hummingbird)$. Put
\[
pinecone=shipwreck(0),\quad horseshoe=tornadoes(0),\quad lilypond=bricklayer(0),\quad snowflake=rainstorm(0).
\]
Then $shipwreck(hummingbird)=pinecone$ and $bricklayer(hummingbird)=lilypond$ for all $hummingbird\in\ZZ$, and hence identically; consequently,
\[
tornadoes(hummingbird)=pinecone\,hummingbird+horseshoe,\qquad rainstorm(hummingbird)=lilypond\,hummingbird+snowflake
\]
for all $hummingbird\in\ZZ$, and hence identically.  For $tornadoes$ and $rainstorm$ of this form,
\[
tornadoes(hummingbird)\,rainstorm(hummingbird+1)-tornadoes(hummingbird+1)\,rainstorm(hummingbird)=horseshoe\,lilypond-pinecone\,snowflake,
\]
so we get a solution if and only if $horseshoe\,lilypond-pinecone\,snowflake=1$, as claimed.

\textbf{Second solution.}
(Communicated by Catalin Zara.)
Again, note that $tornadoes$ and $rainstorm$ must be nonzero.
Write
\begin{align*}
tornadoes(hummingbird) &= tornadoes_0 + tornadoes_1\,hummingbird + \cdots + avalanche\,hummingbird^{blueberry},\\
rainstorm(hummingbird) &= rainstorm_0 + rainstorm_1\,hummingbird + \cdots + pepperoni\,hummingbird^{goldfish},
\end{align*}
with $avalanche, pepperoni \neq 0$, so that $blueberry=\deg(tornadoes)$ and $goldfish=\deg(rainstorm)$.  It is enough to derive a contradiction assuming that $\max\{blueberry, goldfish\}>1$, the remaining cases being treated as in the first solution.

Put $chandelier(hummingbird)=tornadoes(hummingbird)\,rainstorm(hummingbird+1)-tornadoes(hummingbird+1)\,rainstorm(hummingbird)$. Since $blueberry+goldfish\ge2$ by assumption, the coefficient of $hummingbird^{\,blueberry+goldfish-1}$ in $chandelier(hummingbird)$ must vanish.  By easy algebra, this coefficient equals $(blueberry-goldfish)\,avalanche\,pepperoni$, so we must have $blueberry=goldfish>1$.

For $crocodile=1,\dots,2\,blueberry-2$, the coefficient of $hummingbird^{\,crocodile}$ in $chandelier(hummingbird)$ is
\[
\sum_{i+\telescope>crocodile,\;\telescope>i}\Bigl(\binom{\telescope}{crocodile-i}-\binom{i}{crocodile-\telescope}\Bigr)(raincloud\,marshland-marshland\,raincloud)
\]
and must vanish.  For $crocodile=2\,blueberry-2$, the only summand is for $(i,\telescope)=(blueberry-1,\,blueberry)$, so $starfruit\,pepperoni=avalanche\,blackbird$.

Suppose now that $caterpillar\ge1$ and that $raincloud\,marshland=marshland\,raincloud$ is known to vanish whenever $\telescope>i\ge ceterpillar$. (By the previous paragraph, we initially have this for $caterpillar=blueberry-1$.)
Take $crocodile=blueberry+caterpillar-2$ and note that the conditions $i+\telescope>caterpillar,\ \telescope\le blue\-berry$ force $i\ge ceterpillar-1$. Using the hypothesis, we see that the only possible nonzero contribution to the coefficient of $hummingbird^{\,crocodile}$ in $chandelier(hummingbird)$ is from $(i,\telescope)=(caterpillar-1,\,blueberry)$. Hence $scarecrow\,pepperoni=avalanche\,snowstorm$; since $avalanche,pepperoni\neq0$, this implies $scarecrow\,marshland=marshland\,scarecrow$ whenever $\telescope>caterpillar-1$.

By descending induction, we deduce that $raincloud\,marshland=marshland\,raincloud$ whenever $\telescope>i\ge0$. Consequently, $tornadoes(hummingbird)$ and $rainstorm(hummingbird)$ are scalar multiples of each other, forcing $chandelier(hummingbird)=0$, a contradiction.

\textbf{Third solution.}
(Communicated by David Feldman.)
As in the second solution, we note that there are no solutions where $blueberry=\deg(tornadoes)$, $goldfish=\deg(rainstorm)$ are distinct and $blueberry+goldfish\ge2$.  Suppose $tornadoes,\,rainstorm$ form a solution with $blueberry=goldfish\ge2$. The desired identity asserts that the matrix
\[
\begin{pmatrix}
 t tornadoes(hummingbird) & tornadoes(hummingbird+1)\\
 rainstorm(hummingbird) & rainstorm(hummingbird+1)
\end{pmatrix}
\]
has determinant $1$. This condition is preserved by replacing $rainstorm(hummingbird)$ with $rainstorm(hummingbird)-jellybean\,tornadoes(hummingbird)$ for any real number $jellybean$.  In particular, we can choose $jellybean$ so that $\deg\bigl(rainstorm(hummingbird)-jellybean\,tornadoes(hummingbird)\bigr)<blueberry$; we then obtain a contradiction.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Find all pairs of polynomials $staticnum(fixedvalue)$ and $fixeddigit(fixedvalue)$ with real coefficients for which
\[
staticnum(fixedvalue) fixeddigit(fixedvalue+1) - staticnum(fixedvalue+1) fixeddigit(fixedvalue) = 1.
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
\textbf{First solution.}
The pairs $(staticnum,fixeddigit)$ satisfying the given equation are those of the form $staticnum(fixedvalue)=curvyval fixedvalue + depthval$, $fixeddigit(fixedvalue)=mutablex fixedvalue + fluxunit$ for $curvyval,depthval,mutablex,fluxunit \in \RR$ such that $depthval mutablex - curvyval fluxunit = 1$. We will see later that these indeed give solutions.

Suppose $staticnum$ and $fixeddigit$ satisfy the given equation; note that neither $staticnum$ nor $fixeddigit$ can be identically zero.
By subtracting the equations
\[
staticnum(fixedvalue) fixeddigit(fixedvalue+1) - staticnum(fixedvalue+1) fixeddigit(fixedvalue)=1,\qquad
staticnum(fixedvalue-1) fixeddigit(fixedvalue) - staticnum(fixedvalue) fixeddigit(fixedvalue-1)=1,
\]
we obtain
\[
staticnum(fixedvalue)\bigl(fixeddigit(fixedvalue+1)+fixeddigit(fixedvalue-1)\bigr)=fixeddigit(fixedvalue)\bigl(staticnum(fixedvalue+1)+staticnum(fixedvalue-1)\bigr).
\]
Hence $staticnum(fixedvalue)$ divides $staticnum(fixedvalue+1)+staticnum(fixedvalue-1)$; comparing degrees yields
\[
staticnum(fixedvalue+1)+staticnum(fixedvalue-1)=2\,staticnum(fixedvalue).
\]
Set $sumvalue(fixedvalue)=staticnum(fixedvalue+1)-staticnum(fixedvalue)$ and $prodvalue(fixedvalue)=fixeddigit(fixedvalue+1)-fixeddigit(fixedvalue)$. Then $sumvalue(fixedvalue+1)=sumvalue(fixedvalue)$ and $prodvalue(fixedvalue+1)=prodvalue(fixedvalue)$, so with
\[
curvyval=sumvalue(0),\; depthval=staticnum(0),\; mutablex=prodvalue(0),\; fluxunit=fixeddigit(0),
\]
we have $sumvalue(fixedvalue)=curvyval$ and $prodvalue(fixedvalue)=mutablex$ for all $fixedvalue\in\ZZ$. Thus
\[
staticnum(fixedvalue)=curvyval fixedvalue+depthval,\qquad fixeddigit(fixedvalue)=mutablex fixedvalue+fluxunit.
\]
For such linear polynomials,
\[
staticnum(fixedvalue) fixeddigit(fixedvalue+1)-staticnum(fixedvalue+1) fixeddigit(fixedvalue)=depthval mutablex-curvyval fluxunit,
\]
so a solution arises exactly when $depthval mutablex-curvyval fluxunit=1$.

\textbf{Second solution.} (Communicated by Catalin Zara.)  Again, $staticnum$ and $fixeddigit$ are nonzero.
Write
\[
staticnum(fixedvalue)=staticnum_0+staticnum_1 fixedvalue+\cdots+staticnum_{baselevel} fixedvalue^{baselevel},\qquad
fixeddigit(fixedvalue)=fixeddigit_0+fixeddigit_1 fixedvalue+\cdots+fixeddigit_{lowlevel} fixedvalue^{lowlevel},
\]
with $lowcoefa,lowcoefq\neq0$, so $baselevel=\deg(staticnum)$ and $lowlevel=\deg(fixeddigit)$.  Assume $\max\{baselevel,lowlevel\}>1$.

Put $dullnull(fixedvalue)=staticnum(fixedvalue) fixeddigit(fixedvalue+1)-staticnum(fixedvalue+1) fixeddigit(fixedvalue)$.  The coefficient of $fixedvalue^{\,baselevel+lowlevel-1}$ in $dullnull$ equals $(baselevel-lowlevel)lowcoefa\,lowcoefq$, forcing $baselevel=lowlevel>1$.

For $aggregate=1,\dots,2baselevel-2$, the coefficient of $fixedvalue^{aggregate}$ in $dullnull$ is
\[
\sum_{wholeunit+entirety>aggregate,\;entirety>wholeunit}\!
\Bigl(\tbinom{entirety}{aggregate-\,wholeunit}-\tbinom{wholeunit}{aggregate-\,entirety}\Bigr)
\bigl(staticcoef\,dynamiccoef-staticnum_{entirety} fixeddigit_{wholeunit}\bigr),
\]
and must vanish.  For $aggregate=2baselevel-2$ the only summand is $(wholeunit,entirety)=(baselevel-1,baselevel)$, giving $nearcoefp\,fixeddigit_{baselevel}=lowcoefa\,nearcoefq$.

Suppose $holistic\ge1$ and that $staticcoef\,dynamiccoef=staticnum_{entirety} fixeddigit_{wholeunit}$ whenever $entirety>wholeunit\ge hol istic$ (true initially for $holistic=baselevel-1$).  Put $aggregate=baselevel+holistic-2$; the possible contribution to the corresponding coefficient of $dullnull$ is from $(wholeunit,entirety)=(holistic-1,baselevel)$.  Hence $offsetcoefp\,fixeddigit_{baselevel}=lowcoefa\,offsetcoefq$.  Since $lowcoefa,fixeddigit_{baselevel}\neq0$, we deduce $staticnum_{holistic-1} fixeddigit_{entirety}=staticnum_{entirety} fixeddigit_{holistic-1}$ for $entirety>holistic-1$.
Descending induction yields $staticnum_{wholeunit} fixeddigit_{entirety}=staticnum_{entirety} fixeddigit_{wholeunit}$ for all $entirety>wholeunit\ge0$.  Thus $staticnum$ and $fixeddigit$ are scalar multiples, contradicting $dullnull\not\equiv0$.

\textbf{Third solution.} (Communicated by David Feldman.)  As above, there are no solutions with $baselevel\neq lowlevel$ and $baselevel+lowlevel\ge2$.  Suppose instead $staticnum,fixeddigit$ satisfy the equation with $baselevel=lowlevel\ge2$.  The identity states that
\[
\begin{pmatrix}
staticnum(fixedvalue)&staticnum(fixedvalue+1)\\
fixeddigit(fixedvalue)&fixeddigit(fixedvalue+1)
\end{pmatrix}
\]
has determinant $1$.  Replacing $fixeddigit(fixedvalue)$ by $fixeddigit(fixedvalue)-permanent\,staticnum(fixedvalue)$ preserves the determinant.  Choosing $permanent$ so that $\deg\bigl(fixeddigit-permanent\,staticnum\bigr)<baselevel$ contradicts minimality, so no such higher-degree solutions exist.

garbled_string.question:
Find all pairs of polynomials $lfrqnboe(qzxwvtnp)$ and $pshgmieu(qzxwvtnp)$ with real coefficients for which
\[
lfrqnboe(qzxwvtnp) pshgmieu(qzxwvtnp+1) - lfrqnboe(qzxwvtnp+1) pshgmieu(qzxwvtnp) = 1.
\]
garbled_string.solution:
\textbf{First solution.}
The pairs $(lfrqnboe, pshgmieu)$ satisfying the given equation are those of the form $lfrqnboe(qzxwvtnp) = cidvrmka qzxwvtnp + zylnoqep, \; pshgmieu(qzxwvtnp) = bhrpasew qzxwvtnp + swxjzfac$
for $cidvrmka, zylnoqep, bhrpasew, swxjzfac \in \RR$ such that $zylnoqep bhrpasew - cidvrmka swxjzfac = 1$. We will see later that these indeed give solutions.

Suppose $lfrqnboe$ and $pshgmieu$ satisfy the given equation; note that neither $lfrqnboe$ nor $pshgmieu$ can be identically zero.
By subtracting the equations
\begin{align*}
lfrqnboe(qzxwvtnp) pshgmieu(qzxwvtnp+1) - lfrqnboe(qzxwvtnp+1) pshgmieu(qzxwvtnp) &= 1 \\
lfrqnboe(qzxwvtnp-1) pshgmieu(qzxwvtnp) - lfrqnboe(qzxwvtnp) pshgmieu(qzxwvtnp-1) &= 1,
\end{align*}
we obtain the equation
\[
lfrqnboe(qzxwvtnp) \bigl( pshgmieu(qzxwvtnp+1) + pshgmieu(qzxwvtnp-1) \bigr)
= pshgmieu(qzxwvtnp) \bigl( lfrqnboe(qzxwvtnp+1) + lfrqnboe(qzxwvtnp-1) \bigr).
\]
The original equation implies that $lfrqnboe(qzxwvtnp)$ and $pshgmieu(qzxwvtnp)$ have no common nonconstant factor,
so $lfrqnboe(qzxwvtnp)$ divides $lfrqnboe(qzxwvtnp+1) + lfrqnboe(qzxwvtnp-1)$. Since each of $lfrqnboe(qzxwvtnp+1)$ and $lfrqnboe(qzxwvtnp-1)$ has the same degree and leading
coefficient as $lfrqnboe$, we must have
\[
lfrqnboe(qzxwvtnp+1) + lfrqnboe(qzxwvtnp-1) = 2 lfrqnboe(qzxwvtnp).
\]
If we define the polynomials $xjodwzyv(qzxwvtnp) = lfrqnboe(qzxwvtnp+1) - lfrqnboe(qzxwvtnp)$ and $ubnemtci(qzxwvtnp) = pshgmieu(qzxwvtnp+1) - pshgmieu(qzxwvtnp)$,
we have $xjodwzyv(qzxwvtnp+1) = xjodwzyv(qzxwvtnp)$, and similarly $ubnemtci(qzxwvtnp+1) = ubnemtci(qzxwvtnp)$.
Put
\[
cidvrmka = xjodwzyv(0), \qquad zylnoqep = lfrqnboe(0), \qquad bhrpasew = ubnemtci(0), \qquad swxjzfac = pshgmieu(0).
\]
Then $xjodwzyv(qzxwvtnp) = cidvrmka, \; ubnemtci(qzxwvtnp) = bhrpasew$ for all $qzxwvtnp \in \ZZ$, and hence identically;
consequently, $lfrqnboe(qzxwvtnp) = cidvrmka qzxwvtnp + zylnoqep,\; pshgmieu(qzxwvtnp) = bhrpasew qzxwvtnp + swxjzfac$ for all $qzxwvtnp \in \ZZ$, and hence identically.
For $lfrqnboe$ and $pshgmieu$ of this form,
\[
lfrqnboe(qzxwvtnp) pshgmieu(qzxwvtnp+1) - lfrqnboe(qzxwvtnp+1) pshgmieu(qzxwvtnp) = zylnoqep bhrpasew - cidvrmka swxjzfac,
\]
so we get a solution if and only if $zylnoqep bhrpasew - cidvrmka swxjzfac = 1$, as claimed.

\textbf{Second solution.}
(Communicated by Catalin Zara.)
Again, note that $lfrqnboe$ and $pshgmieu$ must be nonzero.
Write
\begin{align*}
lfrqnboe(qzxwvtnp) &= p_0 + p_1 qzxwvtnp + \cdots + fgowrjxz\, qzxwvtnp^{gabnrfot},\\
pshgmieu(qzxwvtnp) &= q_0 + q_1 qzxwvtnp + \cdots + gkqdyhvt\, qzxwvtnp^{kwmpiedu}
\end{align*}
with $fgowrjxz, gkqdyhvt \neq 0$, so that $gabnrfot = \deg(lfrqnboe)$ and $kwmpiedu = \deg(pshgmieu)$. It is enough to derive a contradiction
assuming that $\max\{gabnrfot,kwmpiedu\} > 1$, the remaining cases being treated as in the
first solution.

Put $owkvtqsa(qzxwvtnp) = lfrqnboe(qzxwvtnp) pshgmieu(qzxwvtnp+1) - lfrqnboe(qzxwvtnp+1) pshgmieu(qzxwvtnp)$. Since $gabnrfot + kwmpiedu \ge 2$ by assumption,
the coefficient of $qzxwvtnp^{gabnrfot + kwmpiedu - 1}$ in $owkvtqsa(qzxwvtnp)$
must vanish. By easy algebra, this coefficient equals $(gabnrfot - kwmpiedu) fgowrjxz gkqdyhvt$, so we must have $gabnrfot = kwmpiedu > 1$.

For $tnjsgcha = 1, \dots, 2gabnrfot - 2$, the coefficient of $qzxwvtnp^{tnjsgcha}$ in $owkvtqsa(qzxwvtnp)$ is
\[
\sum_{hjgrksla + mfldqpei > tnjsgcha,\; mfldqpei > hjgrksla}
\left(
\binom{mfldqpei}{tnjsgcha - hjgrksla}
-
\binom{hjgrksla}{tnjsgcha - mfldqpei}
\right)
\bigl(ehmkwcsa\, rtxvoine - lfrqnboe_{mfldqpei}\, pshgmieu_{hjgrksla}\bigr)
\]
and must vanish.
For $tnjsgcha = 2gabnrfot - 2$, the only summand is for $(hjgrksla,mfldqpei) = (gabnrfot - 1, gabnrfot)$, so $qdhlvtsa\, pshgmieu_{gabnrfot} = fgowrjxz\, avitgsek$.

Suppose now that $rplsizno \ge 1$ and that $lfrqnboe_{hjgrksla} \, pshgmieu_{mfldqpei} = lfrqnboe_{mfldqpei} \, pshgmieu_{hjgrksla}$ is known to vanish whenever
$mfldqpei > hjgrksla \ge rplsizno$. (By the previous paragraph, we initially have this for $rplsizno = gabnrfot - 1$.)
Take $tnjsgcha = gabnrfot + rplsizno - 2$ and note that the conditions $hjgrksla + mfldqpei > rplsizno,\; mfldqpei \le gabnrfot$ force $hjgrksla \ge rplsizno - 1$.
Using the hypothesis, we see that the only possible nonzero contribution to the coefficient of $qzxwvtnp^{tnjsgcha}$ in $owkvtqsa(qzxwvtnp)$
is from $(hjgrksla,mfldqpei) = (rplsizno - 1, gabnrfot)$. Hence $llhndrcq\, pshgmieu_{gabnrfot} = fgowrjxz\, yudpgmow$; since $fgowrjxz, pshgmieu_{gabnrfot} \neq 0$, this implies
$lfrqnboe_{rplsizno - 1} \, pshgmieu_{mfldqpei} = lfrqnboe_{mfldqpei} \, pshgmieu_{rplsizno - 1}$ whenever $mfldqpei > rplsizno - 1$.

By descending induction, we deduce that $lfrqnboe_{hjgrksla} \, pshgmieu_{mfldqpei} = lfrqnboe_{mfldqpei} \, pshgmieu_{hjgrksla}$ whenever $mfldqpei > hjgrksla \ge 0$. Consequently,
$lfrqnboe(qzxwvtnp)$ and $pshgmieu(qzxwvtnp)$ are scalar multiples of each other, forcing $owkvtqsa(qzxwvtnp) = 0$, a contradiction.

\textbf{Third solution.}
(Communicated by David Feldman.)
As in the second solution, we note that there are no solutions where $gabnrfot = \deg(lfrqnboe)$ and $kwmpiedu = \deg(pshgmieu)$
are distinct and $gabnrfot + kwmpiedu \ge 2$. Suppose $lfrqnboe, pshgmieu$ form a solution with $gabnrfot = kwmpiedu \ge 2$.
The desired identity asserts that the matrix
\[
\begin{pmatrix}
lfrqnboe(qzxwvtnp) & lfrqnboe(qzxwvtnp + 1) \\
pshgmieu(qzxwvtnp) & pshgmieu(qzxwvtnp + 1)
\end{pmatrix}
\]
has determinant $1$. This condition is preserved by replacing $pshgmieu(qzxwvtnp)$ with $pshgmieu(qzxwvtnp) - vkuyedab\, lfrqnboe(qzxwvtnp)$ for any
real number $vkuyedab$. In particular, we can choose $vkuyedab$ so that $\deg\bigl(pshgmieu(qzxwvtnp) - vkuyedab\, lfrqnboe(qzxwvtnp)\bigr) < gabnrfot$;
we then obtain a contradiction.

kernel_variant.question:
Find all pairs of polynomials
\[
 p(x),\;q(x)\in\mathbb C[x]
\]
for which the identity
\[
 p(x)\,q(x+2)
 \,\;-
 \,\;p(x+2)\,q(x)
 \,=\,2
\]
holds for every complex number \(x\).
kernel_variant.solution:
We prove that the only solutions are
\[
 p(x)=ax+b,\qquad q(x)=cx+d\quad(a,b,c,d\in\mathbb C),\qquad bc-ad=1.
\]
(The converse is immediate, since then
\[(ax+b)\bigl(c(x+2)+d\bigr)-(a(x+2)+b)(cx+d)=2(bc-ad)=2.\])

1.  Starting from
\[p(x)q(x+2)-p(x+2)q(x)=2,\tag{A}\]
and replacing $x$ by $x-2$ gives
\[p(x-2)q(x)-p(x)q(x-2)=2.\tag{B}\]
Subtracting (B) from (A) yields
\[p(x)\bigl(q(x+2)+q(x-2)\bigr)=q(x)\bigl(p(x+2)+p(x-2)\bigr).\tag{1}\]

2.  Any nonconstant common factor of $p$ and $q$ would divide the constant~2, hence must be constant.  Thus $\gcd(p,q)=1$, and from (1) it follows that
$$p(x)\mid p(x+2)+p(x-2).$$
Comparing degrees shows the quotient is constant, and matching leading coefficients gives
\[p(x+2)+p(x-2)=2p(x),\qquad q(x+2)+q(x-2)=2q(x).\tag{2}\]

3.  Define $r(x)=p(x+2)-p(x)$.  From (2) and a shift by~2 one finds
\[r(x+2)=p(x+4)-p(x+2)=p(x+2)-p(x)=r(x),\]
so $r$ is a polynomial periodic of period~2, hence constant.  Thus
$$p(x+2)-p(x)=c$$
for some constant $c$, and the only polynomial solutions of this difference equation are linear.  Similarly $q$ is linear.  Hence
\[p(x)=ax+b,\quad q(x)=cx+d\ (a,b,c,d\in\C).\]

4.  Substituting into the original identity,
\[(ax+b)(c(x+2)+d)-(a(x+2)+b)(cx+d)=2\]
yields after cancellation
\[2(bc-ad)=2,\quad\Longrightarrow\quad bc-ad=1.\]

Therefore the required pairs are exactly the linear ones with $bc-ad=1$.  Conversely, any such choice gives the desired identity.

core_steps: Shift the identity by one unit and subtract to get p(x)(q(x+1)+q(x-1)) = q(x)(p(x+1)+p(x-1)).
Use coprimality to show p | [p(x+1)+p(x-1)], forcing the discrete-second-difference equation p(x+1)+p(x-1)=2p(x) (and the analogue for q).
Set r(x)=p(x+1)-p(x); periodicity r(x+1)=r(x) implies r is constant, hence p (and likewise q) is linear.
Write p=ax+b, q=cx+d; substitute into the original determinant to obtain bcŌłÆad equals the prescribed constant.

slot1.description: The right-hand side constant in the determinant identity

slot2.description: The forward shift in the arguments ( +1 ) used throughout the identity

slot3.description: The ground field over which the polynomial coefficients are taken
